title: Multi-surface distribution: extract knowledge layer + ship as CLI + Claude Code plugin
description:
Inspired by EtienneLescot/n8n-as-code (1049 stars, 4 months old, daily bot regen). Today mediawiki-mcp-server ships as stdio MCP only. The pattern to copy: extract knowledge/logic into a shared package, then ship as MCP server + CLI tool + Claude Code plugin (via marketplace install).

Scope (research bead, not implementation):
1. Inventory what would live in the shared knowledge layer — likely: page-resolution helpers, link-audit logic, terminology/translation rules, MediaWiki API client, formatting converters (markdown ↔ wikitext)
2. Sketch CLI surface — candidates: 'mw publish <file>', 'mw audit-links [--section]', 'mw stale-pages', 'mw search <terms>', 'mw replace <pattern> <replacement>'. The publish-docs skill currently shells out manually; CLI extraction would let it ship as a real tool.
3. Sketch Claude Code plugin manifest — reference EtienneLescot/n8n-as-code/.claude-plugin/ for working manifest.json format. Plugin packaging would let publish-docs + lmwtfy + bid-intel skills ship as installable rather than hand-rolled.
4. Decide bundling pattern: knowledge bundled WITH server (Memtrace pattern, see memtrace-public sift 05-05-2026) vs separate package consumed across surfaces (n8n-as-code pattern). Pick before any code change.

Reference: ~/Documents/remote-v/AI-Knowledge/n8n-as-code - Etienne Lescot.md
Reference: ~/Documents/remote-v/Career/SoMe-Content/Drafts/Substack - Multi-Surface MCP Distribution.md

Definition of done for THIS bead: written design doc in repo root committing to one bundling pattern + CLI surface skeleton + .claude-plugin/ manifest stub. No implementation.

title: wiki grep file fails with HTTP 403 on Tieto wiki — file downloads need session cookie via img_auth.php
description:
\`wiki grep file <Filename> <query>\` returns HTTP 403 when trying to download files from wiki.software-innovation.com:

\`\`\`
~/go/bin/wiki grep file "10 How to Use BPM Tools and Technologies.pdf" "BPM"
Error: grep file failed: failed to download file: download failed with status 403
\`\`\`

## Why this happens

The Tieto wiki uses MediaWiki's authenticated-images feature: file URLs go through \`img_auth.php\` rather than the standard \`/images/...\` path. Observed via successful upload (mediawiki-mcp-server-892 fix) — the upload response returned:

\`https://wiki.software-innovation.com/img_auth.php/f/fc/Wiki_CLI_Test.png\`

Files at \`img_auth.php\` paths require a logged-in MediaWiki session cookie. The \`wiki\` CLI authenticates correctly for API calls (\`api.php\` requests work fine — search, page, history, etc.), but the file download in \`wiki grep file\` likely uses a separate http.Client or a fresh request without forwarding the session cookie.

## Suspected location

Look for the download path in \`wiki/pdf.go\` or \`wiki/read.go\`. The function that pulls a file's binary content to disk (for pdftotext / grep) probably does an unauthenticated \`http.Get(fileURL)\` instead of going through the authenticated client.

The fix is to reuse the same \`c.httpClient\` and \`c.cookieJar\` for file downloads — the session cookie will then ride along automatically.

## Why this matters

Tieto wiki has 4412 images and ~unknown PDFs. None of \`wiki grep file\`, PDF content search, or any "read uploaded file content" workflow works on this wiki today. The same is likely true for any corporate wiki running \`\$wgUploadDirectory\` behind \`img_auth.php\` (a common pattern for SOC-2 compliant deployments).

## Acceptance

- \`wiki grep file <existing PDF on Tieto wiki> <term>\` returns hits (or "no matches"), not HTTP 403
- File download path reuses \`Client.httpClient\` + cookie jar so session auth is preserved
- Smoke test mocking an \`img_auth.php\`-style 403-without-cookie + 200-with-cookie response, asserting cookie is sent
- Public wikis (no auth required) continue to work
notes:
PARTIAL FIX SHIPPED — see below for what worked and what's blocked.

## Code-side fix (DONE)
Client.downloadFile now forwards session cookies from c.httpClient.Jar onto the download HTTP request via req.AddCookie(). The RFC 6265 cookie jar is domain-scoped, so cookies only flow when the download URL is on the same host as the API client (no credential leakage). Regression test TestDownloadFile_ForwardsSessionCookie added asserting 403-without-cookie + 200-with-cookie behavior.

## Tieto wiki still 403s — and it's not our code
Verified with raw curl outside Go entirely:
- Login via bot password (User@BotName format) only sets mediawiki_prod_mw__BPsession cookie
- NOT the regular MediaWiki UserID/UserName/Token cookies that mark a fully-logged-in user
- img_auth.php on this wiki checks for user-level auth ($wgUser->isAllowed('read')) and rejects bot-password sessions

Conclusion: bot password authentication is API-only on this wiki. File reads via img_auth.php need either:
(a) a wiki-admin change to grant the wiki-MCP bot password additional rights (e.g. add a 'High-volume editing' or file-namespace read grant at Special:BotPasswords), OR
(b) switching auth method from bot password to clientlogin (full user session) — bigger refactor, would need User.Password instead of User#BotName.Password

## Status
Code fix is correct and ships value on any wiki that uses regular login sessions. Tieto-wiki-specific 403 is a wiki-admin blocker, separate concern from the Go code. Closing this bead since the code-side fix is complete; opening a follow-up for the auth-method question if/when grep-file on Tieto wiki becomes a priority.

title: wiki upload --file rejected by underlying API: 'local file upload not supported - use file_url instead'
description:
The CLI exposes `--file <path>` and `--url <url>` on `wiki upload`, with `--help` examples showing `--file ./logo.png`. In practice `--file` fails:

```
~/go/bin/wiki upload "Wiki_CLI_Test.png" --file /tmp/wiki-cli-test.png \
  --comment "..." --text "..."
Error: upload failed: failed to read file: local file upload not supported - use file_url instead
```

This means the CLI advertises a flag it can't actually use. Either:
1. The MCP/API path needs to support local file paths (multipart upload), OR
2. The `--file` flag should be removed from the CLI and the `--help` updated to only show `--url`

## Suspected location

`cmd/wiki/upload.go` (flag definitions + dispatch) and `wiki/write.go` or `wiki/upload.go` (server-side handler that returns the "local file upload not supported" string).

## Why this matters

`wiki upload --file` is the natural shape for shell pipelines and CI — you produce an artifact locally, then push it. Forcing callers to host the artifact at a URL first defeats the CLI value-prop ("same API client, same auth, no AI needed"). Cron jobs uploading nightly reports cannot rely on this flag.

## Repro

```
printf '%b' '<minimal-png-bytes>' > /tmp/tiny.png
~/go/bin/wiki upload "Test.png" --file /tmp/tiny.png --text "test"
```

Returns exit-code non-zero, body string above.
acceptance_criteria:
- Either: `wiki upload --file <local-path>` works end-to-end (multipart POST to the wiki API), with a smoke test that uploads a small PNG and confirms File:<name> exists
- OR: `--file` flag is removed from cmd/wiki/upload.go, `--help` only documents `--url`, and the README upload examples are updated to use `--url`
- A `cmd/wiki/upload_test.go` (or extension of errors_test.go) asserts the chosen behaviour
notes: Fixed via Option B: added UploadFileArgs.FileData []byte (json:"-", JSON-hidden for MCP safety). CLI now reads the local file via os.ReadFile and hands the bytes to the wiki client. wiki/write.go split: uploadFromFile keeps the rejected-by-readLocalFile path (preserves MCP-safety stance); uploadFromBytes does the multipart POST. parseJSONResponse also implemented (was a second stub no one had hit before). Verified live against wiki.software-innovation.com: 'wiki upload Wiki_CLI_Test.png --file /tmp/wiki-cli-test.png' uploads a 69-byte PNG, returns the img_auth.php URL. Related finding 'wiki grep file' returns 403 captured as follow-up bead crd.

title: wiki lint exits 0 when terminology findings exist — README says exit 4
description:
The CLI README declares typed exit codes:

| 4 | wiki lint found terminology issues or broken links |

…but `wiki lint "SIF RPC"` against wiki.software-innovation.com produces 17 terminology findings and still exits with code 0. This breaks the documented CI usage pattern — shell scripts checking `$?` for non-zero on lint findings will silently pass.

## Repro (build from main, 15-05-2026)

```
~/go/bin/wiki lint "SIF RPC"
…
17 terminology issues, 0 broken links across 1 page
echo $?
0
```

Expected: exit code 4 because the terminology check produced findings.

## Suspected location

`cmd/wiki/lint.go` — the run* function likely returns nil after printing findings instead of returning a typed exit-code error. Cross-reference with `cmd/wiki/errors.go` (typed exit code definitions) and `cmd/wiki/errors_test.go`.

## Why this matters

The CLI README's Exit codes table is the contract for shell/CI use. Code 4 is reserved specifically for this command's findings — that reservation is meaningless if the command never returns 4. Public docs and behaviour have drifted.
acceptance_criteria:
- `wiki lint <page>` exits 4 when terminology findings exist
- `wiki lint <page>` exits 4 when broken-link findings exist
- `wiki lint <page>` exits 0 when no findings
- `--quiet` and `--json` flags do not change the exit code
- Smoke test in cmd/wiki/errors_test.go (or a new lint_test.go) asserts each of the above branches
notes: False positive — bug report was based on a flawed measurement. `wiki lint "SIF RPC" 2>&1 | head -25` captures `head`'s exit code via `$?`, not `wiki`'s. Verified with redirect-to-file: `wiki lint "SIF RPC" > /tmp/out 2>&1; echo $?` → 4 ✓. The os.Exit(4) call at cmd/wiki/lint.go:174 fires correctly when termIssues>0 or brokenLinks>0. No code change needed. Lesson for future testing: use file redirect (or `set -o pipefail`) when verifying CLI exit codes; never trust $? after a pipe through head/grep/jq/etc.

title: wiki recent: rcstart/rcend swap — same time-window semantic bug as history
description:
Same MediaWiki `rcdir=older`-default semantic mismatch as fixed in `GetRevisions` / `GetUserContributions`. The CLI exposes `--start` / `--end` with user-friendly bound semantics (start = lower, end = upper), but `GetRecentChanges` in `wiki/history.go` maps them directly onto `rcstart` / `rcend`, which under `rcdir=older` mean the opposite (rcstart = newer bound, rcend = older bound).

## Suspected affected callers

- `wiki recent` CLI command (`cmd/wiki/recent.go`) — verify whether it passes --start/--end through.
- The `mediawiki_get_recent_changes` MCP tool.

## Suspected location

`wiki/history.go` — `GetRecentChanges`, lines around 39-45 (the `args.Start` / `args.End` block that sets `rcstart` / `rcend`).

## Why this matters

After mediawiki-mcp-server-qzd ships, "last edited" and "user contributions" lookups are correct but "recent changes" still silently returns the wrong window. The bug surface remains.

## Fix shape

Mirror the qzd fix: swap the param mapping so caller's Start → rcend and caller's End → rcstart. Add equivalent param-mapping assertions to `TestGetRecentChanges_*` tests (audit existing coverage first).
acceptance_criteria:
- `wiki recent --start YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00Z` returns only changes whose timestamp is >= start
- Same for --end
- Same for the `mediawiki_get_recent_changes` MCP tool
- Test in `wiki/history_test.go` asserts the swapped mapping for `GetRecentChanges`
notes: Fixed via the same param-mapping swap as qzd, applied to GetRecentChanges. rcstart/rcend now correctly mapped under default rcdir=older semantics. Acceptance: --start (lower) + --end (upper) work; TestGetRecentChanges_WithAllOptions strengthened to assert swapped mapping (previously encoded the buggy semantic with Start>End); TestGetRecentChanges_EmptyTimeWindow regression test added. Verified live: 7-day window on wiki.software-innovation.com returned 10 changes all within window.

title: wiki history --start filter is ignored — returns full history regardless
description:
The `--start` flag on `wiki history <page>` does not constrain the returned revisions to the requested time window. Output includes revisions from years before the start timestamp.

## Repro (observed 15-05-2026, build from main@f1059fe)

```
~/go/bin/wiki history "360° Integration with MS Teams - Implementation guidelines" \
  --start 2026-04-17T00:00:00Z --limit 50
```

Expected: only revisions on or after 2026-04-17.
Actual: 50 revisions returned, spanning 2026-02-11 back to 2021-06-02. The 4-week window contained zero revisions, but instead of returning an empty/short result, the CLI falls back to unfiltered history.

Reproduced on a second page (`360° Integration with MS Teams`) — same behaviour: revisions from 2023 returned despite a 2026-04-17 start.

## Suspected location

Likely `cmd/wiki/history.go` — the --start flag is parsed but probably not passed into the MediaWiki API call (or the API call doesn't include `rvstart` / equivalent param). The MediaWiki API itself supports time-bounded revision queries; the CLI plumbing is the suspect.

## Why this matters

The CLI is otherwise the right answer for "who edited X recently?" — and the --start filter is the obvious shape for that question. Without it, callers have to post-filter in shell or grep the dates, which defeats the typed-exit-code + --json pipeline story.
acceptance_criteria:
- `wiki history <page> --start YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00Z` returns only revisions whose timestamp is >= start
- Same for `--end` (verify symmetric behaviour while in the area)
- If the window contains zero revisions, the CLI returns an empty result (or a clear "no revisions in window" message) — NOT a fallback to full history
- Add a smoke test in `cmd/wiki/history_test.go` covering both branches
notes: Fixed via param-mapping swap in GetRevisions + GetUserContributions. rvstart/rvend (and ucstart/ucend) now correctly mapped under default rcdir=older semantics. Acceptance: --start lower bound + --end upper bound work, zero-window returns empty (no full-history fallback), regression test added (TestGetRevisions_EmptyTimeWindow). Verified live against wiki.software-innovation.com: 4-week window on '360° Integration with MS Teams - Implementation guidelines' surfaces 2026-05-11 revision that the buggy version hid. Follow-up bead k90 covers the same fix shape for GetRecentChanges.

title: Plugin v0.1.0 release: tag + install test
description: After at least one skill lands (wiki-publish at minimum), tag the plugin release: bump version in .claude-plugin/marketplace.json + plugin.json to 0.1.0, tag the repo, validate /plugin marketplace add olgasafonova/mediawiki-mcp-server end-to-end. Depends on wiki-publish skill being merged. Per the existing release workflow rules (matrix builds, gh release upload, no softprops), the plugin needs no new CI — the manifest is discovered by Claude Code from the default branch at install time.

title: Write wiki-publish skill (first plugin skill)
description: First end-to-end skill in .claude-plugin/skills/. Calls 'wiki publish <file> --json' and reports the revision URL + diff URL. Validates the plugin install path before any other skill is written. See MULTI-SURFACE-DISTRIBUTION.md for the skill contract (every skill shells out to the wiki CLI; no Go re-implementation). DoD: skills/wiki-publish/SKILL.md committed, plugin still loads via /plugin marketplace add olgasafonova/mediawiki-mcp-server, one real publish proven end-to-end.

title: wiki grep file on Tieto wiki — auth method limits us to bot-password sessions; clientlogin or grant change needed for file reads
description:
Follow-up to mediawiki-mcp-server-crd. The code-side fix (cookie forwarding) shipped, but \`wiki grep file\` against wiki.software-innovation.com still returns HTTP 403 because:

1. The client logs in via bot password (User@BotName format). The Tieto wiki sets only a \`mediawiki_prod_mw__BPsession\` cookie on that flow — not the regular UserID/UserName/Token cookies.
2. img_auth.php on the Tieto wiki checks for user-level auth via \$wgUser->isAllowed('read'), and rejects requests authenticated only via bot-password session.
3. Verified outside Go entirely: raw curl with the same login flow + cookies gets the same 403.

## Two paths forward

**Path A — wiki-admin grant change (zero code).** Olga goes to Special:BotPasswords on wiki.software-innovation.com, edits the \`wiki-MCP\` bot password, and adds the file-read grant (likely 'Basic rights' is insufficient — needs to investigate which grant covers img_auth.php). If a granular grant exists, this is the right answer.

**Path B — switch auth to clientlogin (code change).** The client today uses \`action=login\` (the legacy login API designed for bots). Switch to \`action=clientlogin\` (the modern login flow used by browsers), which gives a full user session including UserID/UserName/Token cookies. The downside: clientlogin requires the user's actual password (not a bot password), so the credential surface is broader. Probably needs a config knob to choose between the two.

## Acceptance

Whichever path is taken: \`wiki grep file <PDF> <term>\` against wiki.software-innovation.com returns hits/no-matches, not HTTP 403. Document the choice (and the operational implication for any other wiki running img_auth.php) in README.md.

## Why this isn't crd

crd was about the cookie not being forwarded — that's fixed. This is about the authentication method itself not granting enough rights for file reads on certain wiki configurations. Separate concern, separate fix.
notes: Closed with documented limitation. The bot-password + img_auth.php combination is unsolvable on the CLI side without switching auth methods (clientlogin, which has worse trade-offs around 2FA/SSO/credential surface). Wiki admins on the Tieto wiki won't grant additional permissions to bot passwords. Instead of leaving users staring at a bare 403, downloadFile now detects the specific shape (403 + credentials configured + URL path contains /img_auth.php/) and surfaces an actionable message naming the limitation, the cause, and the workarounds (manual UI download; bot-password grant change at Special:BotPasswords). Two regression tests added: TestDownloadFile_Forbidden_ImgAuthMessage (the message fires) and TestDownloadFile_Forbidden_GenericFor403WithoutImgAuth (generic 403s on other paths get the original message, no false-positive).

title: wiki links --help lists 'orphans' but subcommand is 'orphaned'
description:
Cosmetic but real CLI inconsistency. Running `wiki links --help` shows:

```
Link analysis — external links, backlinks, broken links, orphans
```

…but `wiki links orphans` returns "unknown command". The actual subcommand is `wiki links orphaned`. Users following the parent-help text hit a discoverability dead end.

## Repro

```
~/go/bin/wiki links --help        # parent help, says "orphans"
~/go/bin/wiki links orphans       # error: unknown command for "wiki links"
~/go/bin/wiki links orphaned      # works
```

Also: the parent help omits the `links check` subcommand entirely. The Available Commands section under `wiki links` does list `check` and `orphaned` correctly — the inconsistency is only in the top-level `wiki --help` text and the parent `wiki links` Short description.

## Suspected location

`cmd/wiki/links.go` — the `Short` description on the top-level links command, and possibly the top-level `wiki --help` summary.

## Fix shape

Either rename the subcommand to `orphans` (matches the help) or update the help text to say `orphaned` (matches the command). Mention `check` in the summary too.
acceptance_criteria:
- `wiki links --help` short description names every existing subcommand (currently: backlinks, broken, check, external, orphaned)
- `wiki --help` top-level summary for `links` matches the actual subcommand names
- No subcommand renamed in a way that breaks existing scripts (prefer updating help text)
notes: Fixed: updated the 'links' command Short description from 'external links, backlinks, broken links, orphans' to 'external, backlinks, broken, check, orphaned' — matches the actual subcommand names. Also extended Long description to mention URL reachability checks. Verified locally: both 'wiki --help' and 'wiki links --help' now list correct subcommand names.

title: Add 'wiki stale-pages' and 'wiki similar' CLI subcommands
description: Two missing CLI surfaces flagged in MULTI-SURFACE-DISTRIBUTION.md. MCP tools mediawiki_get_stale_pages and mediawiki_find_similar_pages exist; CLI does not wrap them. Add: 'wiki stale-pages [--days N] [--category X]' wrapping GetStalePages; 'wiki similar <page>' wrapping FindSimilarPages. Both follow the existing cobra command pattern in cmd/wiki/ and support --json + --quiet. Required before wiki-audit skill ships.

title: Refactor tools/handlers.go to green (cc reduction)
description: tools/handlers.go is at Code Health 7.64 (yellow). Three pre-existing complex methods drive it: register (cc=43), registerByName (cc=43), logExecution (cc=24). Path to green (9.1+): split each by category (search/read/write/etc). ~15 small functions replacing 3 monsters. ~250-line diff. CodeScene business case: target 9.1 (top 5%) yields 17-26% time reduction, 26-35% defect reduction at 90% CI. Not architecturally risky — preserves type-safe dispatch, just regroups it. No new dependencies. Tests cover the dispatch logic so refactor is verifiable. Spun out of claude-code-config-0x2.1 (rationale parameter work, 13-05-2026).
close_reason: Refactored: registerByName switch replaced by methodRegistrars map + grouped by category. tools/handlers.go score 7.64 -> 9.53 (yellow -> green).

title: Add tests for wiki/dedup.go
description: 240 LOC deduplication logic with zero test coverage. Found by desloppify portfolio scan. Contains request deduplication for MediaWiki API calls.
